metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify or create the actual script that renders `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` via `envsubst` into `/etc/nginx/conf.d/rebar.conf` (no such script currently exists in `infra/scripts/` or `infra/gerrit/`), and explicitly name it as the artifact this story extends with the deny-all default `opcert-guard.conf` installation step.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the nginx-config install recipe — the documented header of `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` itself (its header documents rendering the template via `envsubst` into `/etc/nginx/conf.d/rebar.conf`; no separate script owns this install today — the recipe IS the owning artifact, and this story edits it as a declared target)' — but a Grep/Read of every script in `infra/scripts/` and `infra/gerrit/` finds NO script that ever calls `envsubst` on `rebar.conf.template` or writes `/etc/nginx/conf.d/rebar.conf`. The header is a comment block, not an executable artifact. The plan proposes to 'extend it with one more line' as though adding a shell command to an existing script, but the owner script does not exist.
findings.location: What section — Compose/nginx paragraph: 'the nginx-config install recipe — the documented header of infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template itself … this story edits it as a declared target'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a new script `infra/scripts/install-nginx-conf.sh` (or extend `install-certbot-timer.sh`, which already installs host nginx and reloads it) to render the template and write the deny-all default guard conf, then explicitly name this script as the target in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify and name the specific script (existing or new) responsible for rendering `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` into `/etc/nginx/conf.d/rebar.conf` and for writing the DENY-ALL default `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf`; add it to `file_impact`.
findings.finding: The plan states the nginx-config install recipe that writes the DENY-ALL default `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` is embedded in `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template`'s header as the 'owning artifact', but no script in the repo actually executes `envsubst` on that template — `install-certbot-timer.sh` installs nginx but does not render the template; `compose-up.sh` never references it. The plan does not identify which script is extended to also write the deny-all default guard file at install time.
findings.location: What section — 'Two ordering hazards are closed explicitly: (a) BOOT' / `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` failure in `compose-up.sh` is fatal to the full compose stack or non-fatal: if the script fails, document whether compose-up.sh continues (leaving /opcert/ fail-closed) or aborts, and add error-handling (e.g., `|| true` / a dedicated guard with a clear log line) to prevent an SSM transient error from blocking Gerrit startup.
findings.finding: The plan wires `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` into `compose-up.sh` to run before `docker compose up`, but does not specify the error-handling behavior if the script exits non-zero at boot time — specifically, whether failure is fatal to the entire compose stack (blocking Gerrit and all other services on the shared VM) or is intentionally non-fatal (leaving /opcert/ in fail-closed 403 state while other services start normally). Given that the stated intent is zero disruption to the existing VM, an unspecified fatal-by-default behavior would contradict it.
findings.location: What section — Compose paragraph: 'It is WIRED INTO the boot orchestrator `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh`... running BEFORE `docker compose up`'
findings.suggested_fix: In compose-up.sh, invoke the materialize script with explicit error handling: `materialize-opcert-guard.sh || { echo 'WARN: opcert guard materialize failed; /opcert/ will return 403' ; true; }` — the DENY-ALL default conf (already installed by the nginx-config recipe) keeps /opcert/ fail-closed, while Gerrit and other services start normally. Document this behavior in the boot-ordering notes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document and implement a failure-handling strategy for materialize-opcert-guard.sh in compose-up.sh: either make the step non-fatal for the broader stack (e.g., log a warning + leave the deny-all default in place so nginx and all other services still start, with /opcert/ returning 403 until remediated) or explicitly acknowledge the hard-fail behavior as acceptable and add a one-line recovery runbook (e.g., 're-run compose-up.sh once SSM is reachable').
findings.finding: The materialize-opcert-guard.sh script is inserted into compose-up.sh as a hard prerequisite BEFORE `docker compose up`, but the plan does not address what happens if the script fails at boot (e.g., SSM GetParameter unavailable due to IAM propagation lag, transient network error, or SSM outage). A non-zero exit here aborts compose-up.sh before any compose service starts, taking down Gerrit and all other existing services. No rollback path or failure-mode handling is described for this scenario.
findings.location: What — Compose section: 'It is WIRED INTO the boot orchestrator infra/scripts/compose-up.sh … running BEFORE docker compose up'
findings.suggested_fix: In compose-up.sh, wrap the materialize-opcert-guard.sh call so that a non-zero exit logs a warning but does not abort the script (e.g., `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh || echo 'WARNING: opcert guard materialization failed; /opcert/ will return 403 until re-run'`). Since the deny-all default is already installed at nginx render time, this keeps Gerrit and all other services running and /opcert/ safely closed. Document the remediation step (re-run the script + nginx -s reload) in the runbook.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the materialize script's error-handling contract: it must run with `set -e` (fail fast), include a timeout on the SSM call (e.g., `aws ... --cli-read-timeout 10`), and on failure leave the existing nginx config in place (fail-closed, not corrupted) — document this contract in the story's What section and assert it in the AC3 test.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify error handling for the `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` script when the `aws ssm get-parameter` call fails (network timeout, expired credentials, permission error): the fail-closed nginx default protects the origin-guard path, but the compose service's own runtime behavior when SSM is unavailable at startup (or mid-rotation) is unspecified, and the materialize script's exit-on-error contract is not stated.
findings.location: ## What (Compose section — materialize-opcert-guard.sh)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either create `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` as a new file (with its schema) in this story's scope, or identify the upstream ticket/artifact that creates it and add it to the `depends_on` chain.
findings.finding: `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` does not exist in the repository (`.rebar/` contains only `cache/`) and is not created by any ticket in the `depends_on` chain; the plan lists it as an edit target in AC4 and `file_impact`, but there is no existing file to pin the public key into.
findings.location: AC4 / `file_impact` entry `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`
findings.suggested_fix: Add `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` to `file_impact` with `reason: 'CREATE — initial trusted environments registry'` and document the expected YAML schema (at minimum: `environments: [{env_id, public_key, added_at_log_position}]`) in the What section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the complete `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` schema (field names, types, list vs. map structure, revocation sub-schema, era-entry shape) in the plan or in a linked artifact, and extend the AC4 test to validate the file structure against that schema — not only the `env_id` field.
findings.finding: `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` is referenced as a deliverable in AC4 (and in the 'Deploy + prove' key-loss-recovery runbook) with named fields (`added_at_log_position`, `env_id`, public key, revocation era entry) but the file does not exist in the repo (`.rebar/` contains only `cache/`) and no schema defining the complete field vocabulary — required fields, types, the revocation sub-structure, whether the file is a YAML list or map, what the `era entry` object looks like — is provided anywhere in the plan. The AC4 test assertion checks only that `env_id` matches `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID`, so a structurally malformed file passes the automated gate.
findings.location: AC4 / 'Deploy + prove' section / `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`
findings.suggested_fix:
Add a 'Schema' sub-section to AC4 (or the What section) showing the minimum valid YAML structure, e.g.:
```yaml
environments:
  - env_id: "<uuid>"
    public_key: "ssh-ed25519 AAAA…"
    added_at_log_position: "<tickets-branch commit SHA at pinning time>"
    revoked_at_log_position: null  # set to tickets-tip SHA on key loss
```
Then extend the AC4 test to parse the file against this structure and assert all required fields are present.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` is invoked as a fatal or non-fatal step in `compose-up.sh`, with explicit rationale (e.g. 'fatal — like `materialize-deploy-key.sh`, because a missing guard would leave the /opcert/ endpoint either unrouted or open without the guard check').
findings.finding: The plan wires `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` into `compose-up.sh` 'BEFORE `docker compose up`' but never specifies whether a failure of the script is fatal (blocking the entire compose boot) or non-fatal (warn + continue). The two existing precedent scripts have opposite error-handling behaviors: `materialize-deploy-key.sh` is fail-closed (exits non-zero, blocks boot), while `materialize-g2p-config.sh` is explicitly non-fatal (wrapped in `if ! bash …; then WARN; fi` at compose-up.sh line 177). An executor has no decision rule to follow and must guess — the wrong choice either makes the box unbootable (if fatal, on any SSM transient) or silently serves 403 on `/opcert/` with no alert (if non-fatal).
findings.location: What section (Compose / boot-orchestrator wiring) / compose-up.sh integration
findings.suggested_fix: Given that a missing guard map means `/opcert/` either 403s (DENY-ALL default) or opens to unguarded traffic, the recommended choice is fatal — consistent with the deploy-key precedent and stated fail-closed intent. Document: 'call as bare `bash infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` (no error-wrapping `if !` guard) so a boot with an unreachable SSM aborts compose-up.sh before docker compose up, matching materialize-deploy-key.sh'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the mechanism for AC3's https-scheme assertion (e.g., 'a `grep` of `infra/terraform/*.tf` for the integration URI prefix, or remove it as already covered by AC1(a)'s jq plan check').
findings.finding: AC3 claims a unit test will assert 'the Terraform integration URI uses the `https://` scheme', but does not specify what artifact or mechanism the test uses for this assertion (TF HCL source grep? plan JSON?). This overlaps with AC1(a)'s jq command against `terraform show -json` but is positioned inside the compose/nginx unit-test block, creating ambiguity about what the AC3 https assertion actually exercises and how it is implemented.
findings.location: AC3, last sentence: 'The test also asserts... that the Terraform integration URI uses the https:// scheme.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the Terraform integration URI uses the https:// scheme' in AC3 with 'a static grep of `infra/terraform/*.tf` confirms the integration_uri value begins with `https://` (cross-check for AC1(a), asserting the source rather than the plan)' — or simply remove it from AC3 with a note 'covered by AC1(a)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a version constraint (e.g. `version = "~> 3.0"`) to the `hashicorp/random` entry in `versions.tf` `required_providers`, matching the lockfile-pinning discipline already applied to `hashicorp/aws`.
findings.finding: `hashicorp/random` is added as a brand-new Terraform provider (`required_providers`) with zero existing call-sites in `infra/terraform/` (Rule-of-Three: 0/3) and no version constraint specified, while the codebase's established pattern for ALL twelve SSM secrets is operator-seeded out-of-band (no generator provider at all). The plan's versions.tf currently pins only `hashicorp/aws ~> 5.0`; the new `random` provider block is described without a version pin, creating a floating dependency that can silently resolve to a breaking major version on the next `terraform init`.
findings.location: What section — 'the `hashicorp/random` provider is ADDED to `infra/terraform/versions.tf` `required_providers`'; AC1 assertion (g)
findings.suggested_fix: In `infra/terraform/versions.tf` `required_providers`, add: `random = { source = "hashicorp/random", version = "~> 3.0" }` (HashiCorp random v3.x is the current stable series); run `terraform init` to generate the `.terraform.lock.hcl` entry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit version constraint (e.g., `~> 3.0`) for `hashicorp/random` in the `required_providers` block in `infra/terraform/versions.tf`, matching the pattern of the existing `hashicorp/aws` constraint.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the `hashicorp/random` provider is added to `required_providers` with no version constraint specified anywhere in the plan text, yet `versions.tf` today only carries `hashicorp/aws ~> 5.0` with a pinned constraint. The plan says '`terraform init` refreshes `.terraform.lock.hcl`' but never specifies what version constraint to add for `random`. Leaving the version constraint open-ended means the resolved version is non-deterministic across environments and `terraform init` calls.
findings.location: What section — Terraform paragraph: 'the `hashicorp/random` provider is ADDED to `infra/terraform/versions.tf` `required_providers` … `terraform init` refreshes `.terraform.lock.hcl`'
findings.suggested_fix: In `infra/terraform/versions.tf` `required_providers`: `random = { source = "hashicorp/random", version = "~> 3.0" }`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `prevent_destroy = true` to the `aws_ssm_parameter` resource for `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` (matching the existing pattern for stateful resources in `main.tf`) to guard the sole-copy trust anchor from accidental `terraform destroy`.
findings.finding: The SSM SecureString `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` is the sole copy of the Ed25519 trust-anchor private key ('the key exists nowhere else') and its loss requires minting a new keypair and a new era entry, yet the plan does not specify `prevent_destroy = true` on this parameter. A `terraform destroy` or accidental targeted destroy would silently drop the trust anchor with no Terraform-level safeguard.
findings.location: What section: 'an SSM Parameter Store SecureString `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` … declared with a placeholder value + lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }'; AC1: '(the key parameter guarded by lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] })'
findings.suggested_fix:
In the Terraform declaration for the key parameter, add:
```hcl
lifecycle {
  ignore_changes  = [value]
  prevent_destroy = true
}
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit key-seeding cleanup step to the deploy procedure: after `aws ssm put-parameter`, securely delete the local private key file (e.g., `shred -u /path/to/key` or equivalent), and update the 'the key exists nowhere else' claim to read 'the key exists nowhere else after the local copy is securely deleted per the runbook'.
findings.finding: The Ed25519 private key seeding procedure instructs the operator to run `ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -N ""` (which writes the key to the local filesystem, e.g. `~/.ssh/id_ed25519`) and then `aws ssm put-parameter` to seed it. The plan states 'the key exists nowhere else', but this is contradicted by the local file created by ssh-keygen. The plan specifies no cleanup step (e.g., `shred -u` or `rm` of the local private key file) after seeding, leaving the private key on the operator's machine indefinitely.
findings.location: Deploy + prove section: 'generate the key locally (`ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -N ""`) and seed the SecureString (the key exists nowhere else)'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the seeding procedure with: `ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -N '' -f /tmp/opcert-ed25519 && aws ssm put-parameter --name /rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key --value "$(cat /tmp/opcert-ed25519)" --type SecureString --overwrite && shred -u /tmp/opcert-ed25519 /tmp/opcert-ed25519.pub`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a default or concrete sourcing instruction for `opcert_admin_principal_arns` in the plan (e.g., 'default = [] — populated at deploy time with the output of `aws sts get-caller-identity --query Arn --output text`; stored in a gitignored `terraform.tfvars`').
findings.finding: The `opcert_admin_principal_arns` Terraform variable has no stated default, example value, or documented source in the plan. An executor must determine which IAM principal ARNs to supply at deploy time with no guidance, which is a blocking gap for the deploy step.
findings.location: What — Terraform section, `opcert_admin_principal_arns` variable; Deploy + prove section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: '`opcert_admin_principal_arns` has no default; at deploy time the operator supplies their own IAM principal ARN (`aws sts get-caller-identity --query Arn --output text`) via a gitignored `infra/terraform/terraform.tfvars` file: `opcert_admin_principal_arns = ["<arn>"]`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Wrap AC1 sub-queries (a) and (e) in `[...] | all(...)` (or `map(...) | all`) so `jq -e` asserts the predicate holds for every matching resource, not just the last.
findings.finding: AC1 sub-queries (a) and (e) use a streaming `select | .field | predicate` pattern with `jq -e`: if the plan contains more than one `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` or `aws_apigatewayv2_route` resource, `jq -e` exits 0 based solely on the truthiness of the LAST emitted value, silently passing even if earlier resources violate the constraint (verify-command defect #1 — substring/partial-match false-positive and #5 — missing cardinality assertion).
findings.location: AC1, sub-queries (a) and (e)
findings.suggested_fix: (a): `[.planned_values.root_module.resources[] | select(.type=="aws_apigatewayv2_integration") | .values.integration_uri] | length > 0 and all(startswith("https://"))` — (e): `[.planned_values.root_module.resources[] | select(.type=="aws_apigatewayv2_route") | .values.authorization_type] | length > 0 and all(. == "AWS_IAM")`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC3 sub-assertion (hermetic) that the nginx-config install recipe emits `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` containing only the deny-all map (`default 0`) — distinct from the materialize script's output — so the fail-closed boot guarantee has a test-verifiable artifact.
findings.finding: The fail-closed DENY-ALL default installation — the extension to the nginx-config install recipe that writes `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` with `map … { default 0; }` before `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` runs — is a described deliverable in 'What' with no covering acceptance criterion. AC3 checks that the nginx route enforces the guard header and that the materialize script renders the SSM value, but neither AC3 nor any other AC asserts that the install recipe (rebar.conf.template rendering step) produces the deny-all default file, leaving the fail-closed boot guarantee untestable against the plan.
findings.location: What section — boot-ordering hazard (a); AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3 with: 'and that the nginx-config install recipe (rebar.conf.template rendering) writes /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf containing `map $http_x_opcert_guard $opcert_guard_ok { default 0; }` (deny-all default), asserted by inspecting the recipe's output in the hermetic test environment.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the duplicate `https://` scheme assertion from AC3 (it is already fully covered by the structured jq sub-check in AC1(a)) and replace it with a distinct AC3-level assertion if needed, or note explicitly that AC3 defers to AC1(a) for this check.
findings.finding: The `https://` integration URI check is asserted twice: once as a structured jq query in AC1(a) against the Terraform plan JSON, and once as a prose assertion in AC3 ('the test also asserts… the Terraform integration URI uses the `https://` scheme'). These two assertions target the same property of the same resource, creating a near-duplicate requirement across two ACs.
findings.location: AC1 sub-check (a); AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Drop 'the Terraform integration URI uses the https:// scheme' from AC3's test list, citing AC1(a) as the covering assertion for that property.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Create a persistent runbook file in the repo (e.g., `infra/docs/opcert-runbook.md` or an entry in the existing `infra/` docs structure) covering: (a) the two-step guard rotation procedure, (b) the key-loss recovery procedure (mint keypair, re-seed SSM, new era entry, revoke old entry), and (c) how to re-run materialize-opcert-guard.sh post-rotation; reference this file from the nginx template header and compose-up.sh comments.
findings.finding: The key-rotation runbook and trust-anchor key-loss recovery procedure are documented only in the story description (ephemeral ticket text), not in a persistent repo artifact. After the ticket closes, operators will have no in-repo reference for these day-2 procedures.
findings.location: What — Deploy + prove section: 'Key-loss recovery runbook (documented in the story's deploy notes)'; What — Compose section: 'the documented runbook is two steps in order'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability deliverables: at minimum, enable API Gateway access logging (`aws_apigatewayv2_stage` `access_log_settings` → CloudWatch log group) in the Terraform plan, add a `restart: unless-stopped` + `healthcheck` to the opcert compose service, and document (or automate) a CloudWatch alarm on 5xx rate or invocation errors — and list these in Scope/In and AC.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no observability (API Gateway access logs, service-level structured logs, or an alert) for the new opcert endpoint; a production failure (e.g., the compose service crashing, the origin guard mismatch after rotation, or API Gateway misconfiguration) would be invisible to operators until a consumer reports a failure.
findings.location: ## What (Compose / deploy sections) / Acceptance Criteria

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback procedure to the story's deploy notes (and reference it in AC5): on partial failure run `terraform destroy -target` on the new resources; on post-deploy rollback revert the compose file and run `terraform destroy` on the API Gateway + SSM + IAM role resources; document that the `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` entry should be removed (or marked revoked) if the deploy is abandoned before the service goes live.
findings.finding: The plan provides a key-loss recovery runbook but no explicit rollback procedure for the infrastructure changes themselves (e.g., if `terraform apply` partially fails mid-run, or if the deployed service proves broken post-deploy and must be quickly removed); the only undo path implied is `terraform destroy` on new resources, but it is not stated, and the compose revert steps are absent.
findings.location: ## What (Deploy + prove section) / Scope

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief decommission note (even one sentence) covering: `terraform destroy` (or targeted `terraform destroy -target`) removes the API Gateway, IAM role, and SSM parameters; the `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` file and the nginx location block should be removed from the host; the era entry in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` should be grandfather-pinned at the retirement log position.
findings.finding: The plan defines no RETIRE/cleanup path for the two SSM SecureString parameters (`/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`, `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard`) or the `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` host file if the opcert service is permanently decommissioned — these persist indefinitely and are not covered by the key-loss recovery runbook (which handles key rotation, not service retirement).
findings.location: What section — the 'What' and 'Scope' sections; key-loss recovery runbook in Deploy paragraph

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` to `file_impact` and specify that `BOT_PATHS` (line 55) must include `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert` so the auto-deploy gate triggers a rebuild when the opcert image changes.
findings.finding: `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh`'s `BOT_PATHS` variable (line 55) is an unenumerated consumer: it drives live redeployment when watched paths change, but does not include `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert` (provided by the sibling story). Changes to that Dockerfile will not trigger an autodeploy, unlike `Dockerfile.reviewbot` which is already watched.
findings.location: `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` line 55 / What section (Compose — extend docker-compose.yml)
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section under 'Compose — extend docker-compose.yml', add: 'extend `autodeploy.sh`'s `BOT_PATHS` to include `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert`.' Add `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` to `file_impact`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Change the `request_parameters` key from `append:header.X-Opcert-Guard` to `overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard` in both the What section and the AC1(b) `jq -e` assertion, so API Gateway unconditionally sets the header to the Terraform-controlled guard value regardless of caller input.
findings.finding: The AC1(b) assertion pins `has("append:header.X-Opcert-Guard")` as the `request_parameters` key for static header injection, but `append:header.<name>` in `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` concatenates a caller-supplied header with the injected value rather than replacing it — the correct key for an authoritative, non-appendable security guard header is `overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard`. With `append:`, a caller who sends `X-Opcert-Guard: junk` receives the concatenated string `junk,<real-guard>` forwarded to nginx; since nginx's `map` performs exact-string matching against the literal guard value, the guard check fails (returns 403 for the manipulated request) — so the mechanism is fail-closed in the attack case — but the converse problem is that a legitimate API Gateway request that happens to include an upstream `X-Opcert-Guard` header (e.g. from a misconfigured client) also gets a multi-value header forwarded, breaking legitimate calls non-deterministically. `overwrite:` unconditionally sets the header to the Terraform-controlled value regardless of caller input, which is the correct security posture for a shared secret.
findings.location: What section, API Gateway integration description; AC1(b) assertion `has("append:header.X-Opcert-Guard")`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `append:header.X-Opcert-Guard` with `overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard` everywhere the key appears (What section description and AC1(b) assertion): `... | .values.request_parameters | has("overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard")`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the claim 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' — HTTP API v2 does support resource-based policies — and replace it with the actual positive rationale for why identity-only is sufficient here (e.g. single-caller scenario, `opcert_admin_principal_arns` variable fully controls trust, resource policy adds operational complexity for no additional security in this threat model).
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' as the exclusive architectural rationale for the identity-policy-only access control design (no `aws_apigatewayv2_api` resource policy in IaC). This claim is factually incorrect — AWS API Gateway HTTP APIs (v2) do support resource-based policies via the `policy` attribute on the API resource. While the identity-only design still works (callers without the role can't produce a valid SigV4 signature that API Gateway accepts for Invoke), the stated rationale is wrong. An implementer reading the plan is told a defense-in-depth option doesn't exist when it does; and if AWS ever changes SigV4 evaluation order, the missing resource policy creates an unguarded fallback. The positive rationale section should state the real reason (cost/simplicity — a resource policy is operationally heavier than a scoped identity policy for a single-caller scenario) rather than a technical impossibility that isn't true.
findings.location: What section: 'Caller restriction is identity-policy-based (an HTTP API has no resource policy)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether AWS API Gateway HTTP API v2 supports resource-based policies (it does as of 2021) and either correct the plan's claim or explicitly document why a resource policy is not used despite being available.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' as the factual justification for restricting callers exclusively via an identity policy on the IaC-created `rebar-opcert-admin` role (no resource policy on the API itself). AWS API Gateway HTTP API v2 has supported resource-based policies since 2021. If the assertion is wrong, the IaC design is missing an available defense-in-depth layer and the stated rationale for the identity-only approach is an unverified capability claim.
findings.location: What section / Terraform subsection: 'Caller restriction is identity-policy-based (an HTTP API has no resource policy)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the correct `request_parameters` key prefix for header injection on `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` (HTTP_PROXY type, HTTP API v2) — confirm whether it is `overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard` or `append:header.X-Opcert-Guard` — and correct the key in both the What section and AC1(b)'s jq assertion.
findings.finding: AC1(b) pins `'append:header.X-Opcert-Guard'` as the `request_parameters` key format for static header injection on the `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` resource, and the What section uses the same key. For HTTP_PROXY integrations on HTTP API v2, the Terraform provider's documented key format for injecting request headers is `'overwrite:header.name'` (not `'append:'`); `'append:'` is used for query strings. If the key format is wrong, the jq assertion in AC1(b) will never match (the actual key in the plan will differ), and the guard header will not be injected into requests reaching the origin.
findings.location: AC1(b): `has("append:header.X-Opcert-Guard")`; What section: 'maps it as a static request-header parameter (`X-Opcert-Guard`) on the API Gateway integration'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `'append:header.X-Opcert-Guard'` with `'overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard'` in both the HCL specification and the AC1(b) jq assertion, consistent with the Terraform AWS provider documentation for `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` `request_parameters`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability for the new API Gateway: declare `access_log_settings` on the `aws_apigatewayv2_stage` resource (logging to a CloudWatch log group) and at least one CloudWatch alarm on the API's 5XXError rate, wired to the existing `aws_sns_topic.alerts` sink.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new internet-reachable API Gateway HTTP API (the trust-anchor service endpoint) but specifies no access logging, CloudWatch metrics, or alarms for it. The existing monitoring infrastructure (monitoring.tf) covers the Gerrit gate but nothing for the opcert API — anomalous 4xx/5xx rates or unexpected caller patterns on the trust anchor would be silent.
findings.location: What section / monitoring gap: 'What' section describes the API Gateway HTTP API; no logging/metrics/alarm clause appears anywhere in the plan for the new API.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `append:header.X-Opcert-Guard` with `overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard` in both the What section description and the AC1(b) `jq -e` assertion, so the static guard header unconditionally overrides any caller-supplied value and the structured test matches the IaC.
findings.finding: The plan's `request_parameters` key `append:header.X-Opcert-Guard` uses the `append:` prefix, which appends to any existing header value rather than unconditionally overriding it. For a security-critical origin-guard header the correct prefix is `overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard` (sets the value regardless of what the caller sent). Using `append:` means a caller who sends their own `X-Opcert-Guard` value will receive a concatenated result at the origin, weakening the guard. Additionally, AC1(b)'s literal assertion `has("append:header.X-Opcert-Guard")` will fail if the implementer (correctly) uses `overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard` — creating a test/implementation mismatch.
findings.location: What section (API Gateway integration description) + AC1 sub-claim (b): `has("append:header.X-Opcert-Guard")`
findings.suggested_fix: In the `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` resource, declare `request_parameters = { "overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard" = random_password.opcert_guard.result }`. Update AC1(b) to: `select(.type=="aws_apigatewayv2_integration") | .values.request_parameters | has("overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard")`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the stated rationale: HTTP API v2 does support resource-based policies; rephrase the design justification to 'identity-policy-only is sufficient and simpler — a resource policy is not added because the IaC-created role and AWS_IAM route auth already restrict callers to the single admin principal' rather than claiming HTTP APIs lack resource policies.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' as the factual basis for the identity-policy-only access control design. AWS API Gateway HTTP APIs (v2) do support resource-based policies (introduced in 2021), so this stated rationale is factually incorrect. While the identity-policy-only approach can still work (AWS_IAM on the route + the IaC-created role is sufficient), the false premise means the design decision is grounded on a wrong capability claim, and readers/implementers may be misled about what security controls are available (e.g., they may not know they could add a resource policy as a defense-in-depth layer).
findings.location: What section: 'Caller restriction is identity-policy-based (an HTTP API has no resource policy)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct or replace the claim 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' with the accurate trade-off framing (HTTP API v2 does support resource-based policies via the `policy` attribute on `aws_apigatewayv2_api`; document the deliberate decision NOT to add one with its rationale, e.g., operator-only callers controlled via trust policy is deemed sufficient).
findings.finding: The plan's architectural rationale for not adding an API Gateway resource policy — 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' — is factually incorrect. AWS API Gateway HTTP APIs (v2) do support resource-based policies (aws_apigatewayv2_api `policy` attribute). By asserting this as a technical constraint rather than a deliberate trade-off, the plan inadvertently forecloses a defense-in-depth layer: a resource policy would prevent any cross-account caller (even one that somehow obtains a valid SigV4 signature from the rebar-opcert-admin role) from invoking the API. The access posture (identity-policy-only) is declared, but the false basis is itself a security concern — future operators may perpetuate the wrong belief that a resource policy cannot be added.
findings.location: What section — API Gateway IaC paragraph: 'Caller restriction is identity-policy-based (an HTTP API has no resource policy)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace: 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' → 'HTTP API v2 does support resource-based policies; this plan deliberately relies on identity-policy-only enforcement (the `rebar-opcert-admin` role inline policy) as sufficient for the single-tenant, operator-only caller model — a resource policy could be added as defense-in-depth if the caller model broadens.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the Terraform state bucket's access policy (who may GetObject on `rebar-tfstate-896586841071`) as part of the security contract for the origin-guard secret, or note that the existing bootstrap IAM controls are sufficient and cite them.
findings.finding: The origin-guard shared secret (`random_password.result`) is stored as cleartext JSON in the Terraform state file (S3 backend with `encrypt = true` for S3-side AES-256 encryption). Any principal with `s3:GetObject` on the state bucket can read the guard value and use it to forge requests directly to the EC2 origin, bypassing the SigV4 layer entirely. The plan never declares who may read the Terraform state bucket or what bucket policy restricts access — the secret's at-rest protection is undeclared beyond S3-side encryption.
findings.location: What section — origin-guard provisioning path; infra/terraform/versions.tf backend config

findings.finding: The AC1 structured `jq -e` assertion verifies only the permission policy (that exactly one `execute-api:Invoke` grant exists labelled `opcert_admin_invoke`) but does NOT provide a structured self-failing test asserting that the `rebar-opcert-admin` role's TRUST POLICY is restricted to `opcert_admin_principal_arns`. A trust policy misconfigured to a wildcard principal (`"Principal": "*"` or `"Principal": {"AWS": "*"}`) would pass all seven AC1 assertions while making the role assumable by anyone.
findings.location: AC1 — structured jq assertions block; What section — `aws_iam_role rebar-opcert-admin` trust policy declaration

findings.finding: The AC1 structured assertions verify the permission policy (exactly one execute-api:Invoke grant with the correct label) but include no structural assertion verifying the rebar-opcert-admin role's trust policy is restricted to opcert_admin_principal_arns. If the trust policy were over-broad (e.g., account-wide 'AWS': '*', or 'AWS': '<account-id>:root'), any IAM principal in the account could assume the admin role and call the API with a valid SigV4 signature — the permission-policy check does not catch this.
findings.location: AC1 — structured jq assertions; 'What' section — 'trust policy limited to the principal ARNs supplied via a new variable opcert_admin_principal_arns'
findings.suggested_fix: Add assertion (h) to the AC1 structured-assertion list: `terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e '[.planned_values.root_module.resources[] | select(.type=="aws_iam_role" and .name=="opcert_admin") | .values.assume_role_policy | fromjson | .Statement[] | select(.Action=="sts:AssumeRole" or (.Action|arrays|contains(["sts:AssumeRole"])))] | length >= 1 and all(.Principal.AWS != "*")'`

findings.finding: The AC1 structured jq -e assertion verifies only the permission policy (that exactly one execute-api:Invoke grant exists with label 'opcert_admin_invoke') but does NOT verify the rebar-opcert-admin role's trust policy is actually restricted to the principal ARNs in opcert_admin_principal_arns. A misconfigured or overly-broad trust policy (e.g., trusting 'Principal: *' or the entire AWS account) would allow any authenticated AWS identity to assume the role and call Invoke — the boundary-crossing sensitive path has an undeclared/unverified access posture on the trust-policy dimension.
findings.location: AC1 — structured jq assertion / 'What' section: 'trust policy limited to the principal ARNs supplied via a new variable opcert_admin_principal_arns'
findings.suggested_fix: In the variable declaration: add `validation { condition = length(var.opcert_admin_principal_arns) > 0 && !contains(var.opcert_admin_principal_arns, '*') error_message = 'opcert_admin_principal_arns must be a non-empty list of specific IAM principal ARNs.' }`. In AC1: add a jq sub-claim such as `terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e '[.planned_values.root_module.resources[] | select(.type=="aws_iam_role" and .name=="rebar_opcert_admin")] | .[0].values.assume_role_policy | fromjson | .Statement[] | select(.Principal | (type=="object" and (.AWS=="*" or .Federated=="*")) or .=="*") | error' 2>/dev/null; [ $? -ne 0 ]`

findings.finding: The `rebar-opcert-admin` IAM role's trust policy (restricting which principals may assume the role via `opcert_admin_principal_arns`) is declared as a security property in AC1 but has no structured, self-failing assertion — the only machine-verifiable jq command in AC1 validates the *permission policy* (that exactly one `execute-api:Invoke` grant exists on the named resource), not the trust policy. An IaC defect that set the trust policy to `"*"` or `"arn:aws:iam::*:root"` would pass the AC1 test command while granting any AWS principal the ability to assume the admin role and invoke the opcert endpoint.
findings.location: AC1 — structured jq assertion; What section — 'trust policy limited to the principal ARNs supplied via a new variable opcert_admin_principal_arns'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the AC1 jq command (or add a second command) such as: `terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e '[.planned_values.root_module.resources[] | select(.type == "aws_iam_role" and .name == "rebar_opcert_admin")] | length == 1 and (.[0].values.assume_role_policy | fromjson | .Statement[] | .Principal | to_entries | all(.value != "*"))'`

findings.finding: The What section describes an explicit, concrete deliverable — the extension of the nginx-config install recipe to write a DENY-ALL default `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` (fail-closed boot behavior) — but no AC verifies this artifact or behavior. AC3 tests the materialize script and the guarded `/opcert/` route, but nothing asserts that a fresh boot before `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` runs returns 403 (not a 500/nginx-start-failure) on `/opcert/`.
findings.location: What — 'boot ordering hazards' paragraph; AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3's test assertion list with: 'the nginx install recipe writes a deny-all `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` before the real materialize step, verified by inspecting the template/install-recipe file for the deny-all map content and the include directive'.

findings.finding: The AC1 structured jq assertion verifies that exactly one `execute-api:Invoke`-granting resource exists and that its Terraform label is `opcert_admin_invoke`, but it does NOT inspect the `Resource` field of the policy statement — a policy with `"Resource": "*"` (invoke on any API) would pass the assertion unchanged, silently violating the plan's own least-privilege claim ('scoped to exactly this API's execution ARN').
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC1 — jq assertion for execute-api:Invoke
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the existing jq pipeline: `and (.[0].s.Resource | if type == "array" then . else [.] end | all(test("arn:aws:execute-api:[^:]+:[^:]+:<API_ID>/")))`; or parameterise with the Terraform output `aws_apigatewayv2_api.opcert.id` substituted at test time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or update CLAUDE.md/the epic design-resolution doc) that formally records the API Gateway HTTP API → public HTTPS origin + shared-secret guard topology, including the explicit rejection of VPC link and the cost/isolation rationale, and list that artifact as a deliverable in the story's Scope/In section.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel integration topology — API Gateway HTTP API (no VPC link) forwarding to a public HTTPS origin protected only by a shared-secret header — and explicitly rejects the VPC-link/Fargate alternative, but no ADR creation or CLAUDE.md update is listed as a deliverable of this story; the referenced ADR 0046 and the epic design resolution address the cost/isolation trade-off but not the specific chosen topology.
findings.location: ## What (Terraform section) / ## Why
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0048-opcert-apigw-public-origin-guard.md` (or the next available number) covering: chosen topology, rejected alternative (VPC link + NLB), cost driver, security properties of IAM-auth + origin guard, and rotation model. Reference it from `infra/terraform/` alongside ADR 0046/0047 references.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document (or implement) atomic file replacement in `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` — write to a temp file then `mv` atomically into place — and note that `nginx -s reload` is graceful (workers retain old config on parse failure), so the fail-safe is already present but should be explicitly acknowledged in the runbook.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify an atomic write for `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` in `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` (no write-to-temp-then-rename pattern described). While concurrent runs are operationally unlikely, a partial write followed by `nginx -s reload` could cause nginx to fail parsing the malformed conf, which would leave the worker processes running the stale (or DENY-ALL) config — but the failure mode is not acknowledged.
findings.location: What section — Compose paragraph: materialize script writes `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf`
findings.suggested_fix: In materialize-opcert-guard.sh: `printf 'map ... { default 0; "%s" 1; }\n' "$GUARD_VALUE" > /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf.tmp && mv /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf.tmp /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf && nginx -s reload`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that the autolander decommission story (ADR 0047 step 3, ticket bcf0) has landed on main before citing the 'freed compose slot' as a verified capacity fact, or reframe the assertion as 'no autolander service is present in docker-compose.yml as of this story's base'.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that the `opcert` compose service rides 'the compose slot freed by the autolander's retirement, ADR 0047'. Reading `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml`, the file has exactly two services (`gerrit` and `review-bot`) — there is no autolander service present. ADR 0047's migration step 2 is 'Delete the auto-lander code' and step 3 is 'Decommission the deployed infra', but no autolander Dockerfile or compose service entry exists in the current codebase. The 'freed slot' premise cannot be verified from the repo, and the docker-compose.yml does not confirm a slot was freed — it simply has no autolander entry.
findings.location: Why section: 'the compose slot freed by the autolander's retirement, ADR 0047'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify why `hashicorp/random` (`random_password`) is preferred over the codebase's established out-of-band generate + `aws ssm put-parameter --overwrite` + `lifecycle { ignore_changes }` pattern (used for all 12 existing SSM secrets), or replace it with that pattern to avoid a new provider dependency.
findings.finding: `hashicorp/random` (`random_password`) is introduced as a brand-new Terraform provider with zero existing call-sites in `infra/terraform/` (Rule-of-Three: 0/3) solely to generate the origin-guard value, while the codebase's established prior art for all 12 existing SSM secrets (ssm.tf lines 60-74) is out-of-band generation + `aws ssm put-parameter --overwrite` + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` — a pattern the plan itself applies to the Ed25519 key in the same story. A new provider introduces a lock-file entry, a `terraform init` dependency, and additional Terraform state, when an operationally equivalent approach (generate guard with `openssl rand -hex 32` out-of-band, seed SSM, protect with `ignore_changes`) already exists in the codebase and requires no new provider.
findings.location: What section / Terraform subsection — origin-guard provisioning path; `infra/terraform/versions.tf`
findings.suggested_fix: Generate the origin-guard value out-of-band (e.g. `openssl rand -hex 32`) and seed `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard` the same way the Ed25519 key is seeded; declare the SSM parameter with `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` and read it back via a `data "aws_ssm_parameter"` source to inject into the API Gateway integration — eliminating the `hashicorp/random` provider entirely and keeping the codebase's single seeding convention.

findings.finding: The `jq` path `.lifecycle.ignore_changes?` in AC1(f) is wrong for `terraform show -json` output. In the `configuration` section of `terraform show -json` (Terraform ≥ 1.0), lifecycle meta-arguments are nested under `.lifecycle_meta_arguments`, not `.lifecycle`. With the current path, `.lifecycle.ignore_changes?` returns `null` (field absent), `// empty` substitutes `empty` for null, and `empty | length > 0` produces no jq output — causing `jq -e` to exit with code 5 (no output path) even when `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` IS correctly declared. The proving command is a permanent false-negative: it fails on a correct plan and trivially passes if the lifecycle block is omitted (by inverting the test logic).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 sub-claim (f)
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the AC1(f) sub-claim command with:
```
terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e '
  .configuration.root_module.resources[]
  | select(.address=="aws_ssm_parameter.opcert_ed25519_key")
  | .lifecycle_meta_arguments.ignore_changes?
  // empty
  | length > 0
'
```
This correctly targets the `lifecycle_meta_arguments` object that Terraform's JSON output places lifecycle directives in under the `configuration` section.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
